title: Today's AI Brief: Check out real-life AI prototypes from the Futures Lab
summary: Exploring AI prototypes from Google's Futures Lab and the future of physical AI interfaces
imageAlt: Image from source article for Check out real-life AI prototypes from the Futures Lab.
imageCaption: Check out real-life AI prototypes from the Futures Lab.
imageSourceTitle: Check out real-life AI prototypes from the Futures Lab.

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's AI Brief. I've been digging into some fascinating developments in the world of artificial intelligence, and I'm excited to share them with you. I've got a couple of stories that caught my attention recently, and I think you'll find them pretty interesting.

First up, Google's Futures Lab has been working on some amazing AI prototypes. Students funded by Google have created custom AI-generated stories to help learn languages like Japanese. Imagine being able to practice conversing in Japanese with a personalized story tailored just for you. That's what these students have built. They've also developed an AI tutor that gives real-time feedback on sign language practice. It's incredible to see AI being used to make learning more accessible and effective.

Another interesting development is the future of physical AI interfaces. According to an article on IEEE Spectrum, the focus isn't just on making smarter robots, but on creating smarter interfaces that allow humans and machines to interact more seamlessly. Think about a field technician working on a wind turbine - they need to send commands to a diagnostic device quickly and easily. The article explores how advancements in interfaces are changing the game for physical AI. It's not just about making robots smarter, but about making the interactions between humans and machines more efficient.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
